dialogues.text: Here are four pictures. Three of them show equal groups. One does NOT. Which one is NOT equal groups?

Those two boxes have different amounts: 3 in one, 5 in the other. That's not equal. For groups to be EQUAL, every container needs the same number inside. The other three all have the same number in each group. That's what makes them equal groups.
Three different pictures. Take a look at each one. What do all three pictures show?

All three show 3 groups of 5. Bags, circles, graph symbols — different containers, same structure inside. That's what multiplication IS. It describes the same pattern no matter how the groups look. 3 groups of 5 is always 3 × 5 — whether it's bags on a shelf, dots in circles, or symbols on a graph.
Equal groups aren't just in math problems. Here's a real situation.
Can you build what the shelf looks like?
How many in each pack? Add the juice boxes.
4 packs, 6 in each. Equal groups — right there on a store shelf.
4 groups of 6. That's 4 × 6. Any time you see the same number in each container — packs, bags, boxes, cartons — that's equal groups. It's multiplication, even outside math class.
Here's something to think about. Today's work connects to something you've been doing for a while. Did you notice a connection?
You spotted it. Every time you read a scale of 5 graph, you were counting groups of 5. Graph scales were equal groups the whole time.
Good noticing. The counting by 5s and counting by 2s from graphs — that was groups all along. The connection gets clearer over time.
That makes sense — the multiplication symbol and equal groups are new. But remember the Warmup graph? Those symbols were holding groups inside. You'll see more connections as we keep going.
